---
title: "Session 12: Quarto Outputs"
subtitle: "R Programming Workbook"
author: "R Programming Course"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    theme: cosmo
    self-contained: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Learning Objectives

::: {.callout-note title="Learning Objectives"}
By the end of this session you will be able to:

1. Create Reveal.js presentations in Quarto with incremental reveals, speaker notes, and custom themes
2. Use presentation-specific features like columns, fragments, and background images
3. Build interactive dashboards using Quarto's dashboard layout system
4. Arrange dashboard components with rows, columns, cards, and value boxes
5. Add interactive widgets to dashboards using plotly and leaflet
6. Set up a Quarto website project with navigation and multiple pages
7. Create a blog with listings, categories, and date-based organization
8. Configure website navigation, themes, and deployment options
:::

## Part 1: Quarto Presentations with Reveal.js

Quarto can produce presentation slides in multiple formats. Reveal.js gives you the most control and interactivity for HTML-based presentations.

### Minimal Reveal.js Presentation

The simplest Reveal.js document:

````{verbatim}
---
title: "My First Slides"
format: revealjs
---

## Slide One

Content goes here.

## Slide Two

More content.
````

Each level-2 heading (`##`) creates a new slide. The title page is generated automatically from the YAML.

### Slide Structure and Layout

Reveal.js supports horizontal and vertical slides:

````{verbatim}
---
title: "Slide Structure"
format: revealjs
---

## Horizontal Slide 1

Use ## for horizontal navigation.

## Horizontal Slide 2

### Vertical Slide 2a

Use ### for vertical (nested) slides.

### Vertical Slide 2b

Navigate down from Slide 2.
````

Horizontal slides move left/right. Vertical slides move up/down under their parent.

### Incremental Content

Reveal content piece by piece:

````{verbatim}
## Incremental List

::: {.incremental}
- First point appears
- Second point appears
- Third point appears
:::

## Pause Between Content

First paragraph shows.

. . .

Second paragraph appears after a click.

. . .

Third paragraph appears after another click.
````

The `. . .` syntax (three dots with spaces) creates a pause. The `.incremental` class makes list items appear one at a time.

### Speaker Notes

Add notes visible only in speaker view (press `S` during presentation):

````{verbatim}
## Important Slide

Key point for the audience.

::: {.notes}
This text only appears in speaker view.
Remind students about the assignment deadline.
Estimated time: 5 minutes on this slide.
:::
````

### Your Turn: Basic Presentation

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Create a short Reveal.js presentation about a topic you know well (a hobby, a programming language, a recipe). Include:

- A title slide
- At least 4 content slides
- One slide with incremental content
- Speaker notes on at least 2 slides

Write the YAML and markdown below:

```{r}
#| eval: false

# Write your .qmd content as a string to see the structure:
presentation_content <- '
---
title: "Your Title"
format: revealjs
---

## Slide 1

Your content here.
'

cat(presentation_content)
```
:::

### Columns and Layout

Split slides into columns:

````{verbatim}
## Two Column Layout

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
Left column content.

- Point A
- Point B
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
Right column content.

```{r}
plot(1:10)
```
:::

::::
````

### Code on Slides

Control how code appears:

````{verbatim}
## Code Display Options

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| code-line-numbers: "2-3"
#| output-location: fragment

library(ggplot2)
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 3) +
  theme_minimal()
```
````

`code-line-numbers` highlights specific lines. `output-location: fragment` shows output on click.

### Slide Backgrounds and Themes

````{verbatim}
---
title: "Styled Presentation"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: dark
    slide-number: true
    transition: slide
    chalkboard: true
---

## Normal Slide

Regular background.

## {background-color="navy"}

This slide has a navy background.

## {background-image="https://example.com/image.jpg"}

This slide has an image background.
````

Available themes: `default`, `dark`, `moon`, `night`, `serif`, `simple`, `sky`, `solarized`.

### Your Turn: Styled Presentation

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Enhance your presentation from the previous exercise:

1. Add a two-column slide comparing two things
2. Add a code chunk with `output-location: fragment`
3. Change the theme to something other than default
4. Add `slide-number: true` to the YAML

```{r}
#| eval: false

# Update your presentation content:
styled_presentation <- '
---
title: "Enhanced Presentation"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: moon
    slide-number: true
---

## Your slides here
'

cat(styled_presentation)
```
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Common Mistake"}
Forgetting the four-colon `::::` fence when nesting div blocks:

Wrong:
````{verbatim}
::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
Content
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
Content
:::
:::
````

The outer fence needs four colons `::::` to distinguish it from the inner three-colon fences. Quarto will silently produce broken layout without an error.
:::

### Fragments and Custom Classes

Control animation of individual elements:

````{verbatim}
## Fragment Styles

::: {.fragment .fade-in}
I fade in.
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
I fade up.
:::

::: {.fragment .highlight-red}
I turn red.
:::

::: {.fragment .strike}
I get struck through.
:::
````

Fragment styles: `fade-in`, `fade-out`, `fade-up`, `fade-down`, `fade-left`, `fade-right`, `highlight-red`, `highlight-blue`, `highlight-green`, `strike`, `semi-fade-out`.

### Your Turn: Complete Presentation

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Build a 6-slide Reveal.js presentation that reports on the `mtcars` dataset. Include:

1. Title slide with your name
2. Overview slide describing the dataset (use incremental list)
3. Two-column slide: summary stats on left, scatter plot on right
4. A slide with a code chunk and `output-location: fragment`
5. A slide with speaker notes
6. A closing slide with key findings

```{r}
#| eval: false

# Write the full presentation structure here:
mtcars_presentation <- '
---
title: "Exploring mtcars"
author: "Your Name"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: dark
    slide-number: true
    code-line-numbers: true
---

## Overview

Your content...
'

cat(mtcars_presentation)
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Challenge"}
Add a custom SCSS theme file reference to your presentation YAML. The SCSS should change the heading font, background color, and link color. Write the CSS rules you would put in `custom.scss`:

```{r}
#| eval: false

custom_scss <- '
/*-- scss:defaults --*/
$presentation-heading-font: "Georgia", serif;
$body-bg: #1a1a2e;
$link-color: #e94560;

/*-- scss:rules --*/
.reveal .slide-number {
  font-size: 0.7em;
  color: #e94560;
}
'
cat(custom_scss)
```
:::

## Part 2: Quarto Dashboards

Quarto dashboards let you create interactive data displays without Shiny. They use a card-based layout system.

### Minimal Dashboard

````{verbatim}
---
title: "My Dashboard"
format: dashboard
---

## Row

```{r}
plot(1:10, main = "Simple Plot")
```

```{r}
summary(mtcars)
```
````

The `format: dashboard` setting changes how content is arranged. Each code chunk becomes a card. Level-2 headings define rows.

### Dashboard Layout: Rows and Columns

````{verbatim}
---
title: "Layout Example"
format: dashboard
---

## Row {height="40%"}

```{r}
#| title: "Sales Over Time"
plot(1:100, cumsum(rnorm(100)), type = "l")
```

## Row {height="60%"}

### Column {width="60%"}

```{r}
#| title: "Revenue by Region"
barplot(c(North = 340, South = 280, West = 420))
```

### Column {width="40%"}

```{r}
#| title: "Summary Stats"
summary(mtcars[, 1:4])
```
````

Rows stack vertically. Columns within rows stack horizontally. Use `{height="X%"}` and `{width="X%"}` to control sizes.

### Value Boxes

Display key metrics:

````{verbatim}
## Row {height="20%"}

```{r}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Total Revenue"
#| icon: currency-dollar
#| color: success
list(value = "$1.2M")
```

```{r}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Orders"
#| icon: cart
#| color: primary
list(value = "4,521")
```

```{r}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Return Rate"
#| icon: arrow-return-left
#| color: warning
list(value = "4.8%")
```
````

Value boxes use Bootstrap icons. Colors: `primary`, `secondary`, `success`, `danger`, `warning`, `info`, `light`, `dark`.

### Your Turn: Simple Dashboard

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Design a dashboard for the `mtcars` dataset with:

- A row of 3 value boxes showing: number of cars, average MPG, average horsepower
- A row with two charts: scatter plot of mpg vs wt, and bar chart of cars per cylinder count

Write the dashboard YAML and content:

```{r}
#| eval: false

dashboard_content <- '
---
title: "mtcars Dashboard"
format: dashboard
---

## Row {height="20%"}

Your value boxes here...

## Row {height="80%"}

Your charts here...
'

cat(dashboard_content)
```
:::

### Tabsets in Dashboards

Tabs let you pack more content into the same space:

````{verbatim}
## Row {.tabset}

```{r}
#| title: "Plot View"
plot(mtcars$wt, mtcars$mpg)
```

```{r}
#| title: "Table View"
head(mtcars)
```

```{r}
#| title: "Summary"
summary(mtcars)
```
````

Adding `{.tabset}` to a row heading turns its children into tabs instead of side-by-side cards.

### Pages in Dashboards

Create multi-page dashboards:

````{verbatim}
---
title: "Multi-Page Dashboard"
format: dashboard
---

# Overview

## Row

```{r}
#| title: "Main Chart"
plot(1:10)
```

# Details

## Row

```{r}
#| title: "Detailed Table"
head(mtcars, 20)
```
````

Level-1 headings (`#`) create pages. Navigation tabs appear at the top.

### Interactive Widgets

Add interactivity without Shiny using htmlwidgets:

```{r}
#| eval: false

# plotly for interactive plots
library(plotly)
p <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, color = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  theme_minimal()
ggplotly(p)

# DT for interactive tables
library(DT)
datatable(mtcars, filter = "top", options = list(pageLength = 10))

# leaflet for maps
library(leaflet)
leaflet() |>
  addTiles() |>
  addMarkers(lng = -73.99, lat = 40.73, popup = "NYC")
```

These widgets work in dashboards without a server.

### Your Turn: Multi-Page Dashboard

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Design a two-page dashboard:

Page 1 "Overview": value boxes + a main chart
Page 2 "Details": a tabset with plot, table, and summary views

```{r}
#| eval: false

multi_page <- '
---
title: "Car Analysis"
format: dashboard
---

# Overview

## Row {height="20%"}

Value boxes...

## Row {height="80%"}

Main chart...

# Details

## Row {.tabset}

Tab content...
'

cat(multi_page)
```
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Common Mistake"}
Using regular R output in value boxes instead of a list:

Wrong:
````{verbatim}
```{r}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Count"
nrow(mtcars)
```
````

Right:
````{verbatim}
```{r}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Count"
list(value = nrow(mtcars))
```
````

Value boxes expect `list(value = ...)`. A bare value will not render correctly.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Challenge"}
Design a dashboard that uses the `sales` dataset from Sessions 5-10. Include:

- 4 value boxes: total revenue, total units, return rate, number of products
- A row with a time series chart and a bar chart by region
- A tabset row with tables filtered by product

Write the complete dashboard structure.
:::

## Part 3: Quarto Websites and Blogs

Quarto websites let you publish multiple connected pages with shared navigation.

### Website Project Structure

A Quarto website is a project with a `_quarto.yml` configuration file:

```
my-website/
  _quarto.yml
  index.qmd
  about.qmd
  analysis.qmd
  styles.css
```

The `_quarto.yml` file defines the site:

````{verbatim}
project:
  type: website

website:
  title: "My Research Site"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: about.qmd
        text: About
      - href: analysis.qmd
        text: Analysis

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    css: styles.css
````

### Creating Pages

Each `.qmd` file becomes a page:

````{verbatim}
---
title: "Home"
---

Welcome to my research site.

## Recent Work

See the [analysis](analysis.qmd) page for results.
````

Links between pages use relative paths to `.qmd` files. Quarto converts them to `.html` links on render.

### Navigation Options

Quarto websites support navbar and sidebar navigation:

````{verbatim}
# Navbar with dropdown
website:
  navbar:
    left:
      - text: "Data"
        menu:
          - href: data-raw.qmd
            text: "Raw Data"
          - href: data-clean.qmd
            text: "Clean Data"
    right:
      - icon: github
        href: https://github.com/username/repo

# Sidebar navigation
website:
  sidebar:
    style: docked
    contents:
      - section: "Getting Started"
        contents:
          - index.qmd
          - setup.qmd
      - section: "Analysis"
        contents:
          - analysis.qmd
          - results.qmd
````

### Your Turn: Website Configuration

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Write the `_quarto.yml` for a course project website with:

- A navbar with: Home, Methods, Results, About
- A dropdown under "Results" for: Tables and Figures
- A GitHub icon link in the right navbar
- The `flatly` theme

```{r}
#| eval: false

quarto_yml <- '
project:
  type: website

website:
  title: "Your Project"
  navbar:
    left:
      # Your navigation here
    right:
      # GitHub icon here

format:
  html:
    theme: flatly
'

cat(quarto_yml)
```
:::

### Blog Setup

A Quarto blog is a website with a listing page:

````{verbatim}
# _quarto.yml
project:
  type: website

website:
  title: "Data Science Blog"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Blog
      - href: about.qmd
        text: About
````

The blog index page uses a listing:

````{verbatim}
---
title: "My Blog"
listing:
  contents: posts
  sort: "date desc"
  type: default
  categories: true
  feed: true
---
````

Blog posts go in a `posts/` directory:

```
my-blog/
  _quarto.yml
  index.qmd
  about.qmd
  posts/
    2024-01-15-first-post/
      index.qmd
      image.png
    2024-02-01-second-post/
      index.qmd
```

Each post has its own folder with an `index.qmd`:

````{verbatim}
---
title: "My First Post"
author: "Your Name"
date: "2024-01-15"
categories: [R, tutorial]
image: "image.png"
---

Post content goes here.
````

### Listing Options

Control how the blog listing appears:

````{verbatim}
---
title: "Blog"
listing:
  contents: posts
  sort: "date desc"
  type: grid          # default, grid, or table
  categories: true
  feed: true
  page-size: 5
  fields: [title, author, date, categories, description]
---
````

Three listing types: `default` (list view), `grid` (card view), `table` (spreadsheet view).

### Your Turn: Blog Structure

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Plan a blog about your R learning journey. Write:

1. The `_quarto.yml` configuration
2. The `index.qmd` listing page (using grid layout)
3. The YAML front matter for two blog posts with categories

```{r}
#| eval: false

blog_yml <- '
project:
  type: website

website:
  title: "Your Blog Title"
  # Add navbar
'

blog_index <- '
---
title: "Your Blog"
listing:
  # Configure listing
---
'

post1_yaml <- '
---
title: "First Post Title"
# Add metadata
---
'

cat(blog_yml, "\n---\n", blog_index, "\n---\n", post1_yaml)
```
:::

### Website Themes and Customization

Apply built-in themes or customize with CSS/SCSS:

````{verbatim}
# In _quarto.yml
format:
  html:
    theme:
      light: flatly
      dark: darkly
    css: custom.css
    fontsize: 1.1em
    linestretch: 1.6
````

The `light`/`dark` setup gives users a theme toggle.

Custom CSS goes in a separate file:

```{r}
#| eval: false

custom_css <- '
/* custom.css */
.quarto-title {
  color: #2c3e50;
  border-bottom: 3px solid #3498db;
}

.sidebar nav {
  font-size: 0.9em;
}

code {
  background-color: #f0f0f0;
  padding: 2px 5px;
  border-radius: 3px;
}
'
cat(custom_css)
```

### Rendering and Preview

In Positron, render a website project:

```{r}
#| eval: false

# Render entire site
# Terminal: quarto render

# Preview with live reload
# Terminal: quarto preview

# Render single page
# Terminal: quarto render analysis.qmd
```

The `_site/` directory contains the rendered output. This is what you deploy.

::: {.callout-warning title="Common Mistake"}
Putting absolute file paths in website links:

Wrong: `[Results](/Users/me/project/results.qmd)`

Right: `[Results](results.qmd)`

Always use relative paths. Absolute paths break when the site is deployed to a different location.
:::

### Your Turn: Complete Website Plan

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Design a complete Quarto website for a data analysis project. Write:

1. The directory structure (list all files and folders)
2. The `_quarto.yml` with sidebar navigation
3. The `index.qmd` home page with a welcome message and links to other pages
4. The YAML for a results page that includes code chunks

```{r}
#| eval: false

# 1. Directory structure
dir_structure <- "
project-site/
  _quarto.yml
  index.qmd
  ...add more files...
"

# 2. _quarto.yml
quarto_config <- '
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: _site

website:
  title: "Project Name"
  sidebar:
    style: docked
    contents:
      # Your navigation
'

# 3. index.qmd
home_page <- '
---
title: "Welcome"
---

Your home page content...
'

cat(dir_structure, "\n---\n", quarto_config, "\n---\n", home_page)
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Challenge"}
Create a full blog post in Quarto that:

1. Analyzes the `mtcars` dataset
2. Includes at least 2 ggplot2 visualizations
3. Uses cross-references for figures
4. Has categories and a description in the YAML
5. Uses tabsets to show code and output separately

Write the complete post content.
:::

## Session Synthesis

### Connect the Dots

::: {.callout-important title="Synthesis Exercise"}
You are building a portfolio to showcase your R skills. Design a Quarto website that includes:

1. **Home page**: Brief intro and links to your projects
2. **Blog section**: With at least 2 posts about R topics you have learned
3. **Dashboard page**: An embedded dashboard analyzing a dataset
4. **Presentation page**: A link to a Reveal.js presentation you created

Write out:

- The complete `_quarto.yml`
- The directory structure
- The YAML front matter for each page
- The listing configuration for the blog
- One complete blog post with code and visualizations

```{r}
#| eval: false

# Your complete portfolio plan:
portfolio_config <- '
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: _site

website:
  title: "My R Portfolio"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: blog.qmd
        text: Blog
      - href: dashboard.qmd
        text: Dashboard
      - href: presentation.qmd
        text: Slides
    right:
      - icon: github
        href: https://github.com/yourusername

format:
  html:
    theme:
      light: flatly
      dark: darkly
'
cat(portfolio_config)
```
:::

### Key Takeaways

1. Reveal.js presentations use `##` for slides, `. . .` for pauses, and `::: {.notes}` for speaker notes
2. Dashboards use `format: dashboard` with rows, columns, value boxes, and tabsets arranged by headings
3. Websites need a `_quarto.yml` file defining project type, navigation, and shared format settings
4. Blogs are websites with listing pages that auto-generate post indexes from a `posts/` directory
5. All Quarto output types share the same markdown and code chunk syntax, just different YAML and layout rules

### Prepare for Next Session

Next session covers advanced Quarto: custom chunk options, conditional content, extensions, custom formats, and publishing workflows. Before next session:

- Experiment with rendering a simple Reveal.js deck in Positron
- Try creating a `_quarto.yml` file and rendering a two-page website
- Browse the Quarto gallery at <https://quarto.org/docs/gallery/> for inspiration
